statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers, determine whether any triple of elements forms a duplicate rotation. A duplicate rotation exists if there exists a starting index and rotation count such that the rotated triple at that index and count is found at least twice in the array. For example, if the input array is [2, 1, 3, 3, 1, 2], the function should return True because the rotation [1, 2, 3] appears twice in the array.

The function should return False for the following input arrays: [1, 3, 2, 4, 5] and [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a list of integers where each number is between 1 and 1000, inclusive. The first element in the input list will represent the size of the array, followed by the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a boolean value indicating whether a duplicate rotated triple exists in the array.
statement.notes: This is a medium difficulty problem that requires a combination of problem-solving skills and coding skills. The time complexity of the solution should be O(n^2). The space complexity can be O(1) if we only store the necessary variables.

examples.explanation: The input array contains the duplicate rotation [1, 3, 2] at the start and end.
There are no duplicate rotations in the input array.
The array contains no duplicate rotations.

hints.text: Start by sorting the input array. This will make it easier to find duplicate rotations.
Instead of using nested loops to find the rotations, use the two-pointer technique to find the first occurrence of each rotation.

editorial.title: Correctly Identifying Duplicate Rotated Triples
editorial.intuition: The key to solving this problem is to recognize that duplicate rotations will always appear at the start and end of the input array. Therefore, we can first sort the array to group rotations together and then use the two-pointer technique to find the first occurrence of each rotation.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to use nested loops to find each rotation in the input array and then check if it appears more than once. This approach has a time complexity of O(n^3).
editorial.betterSolution: We can improve the time complexity to O(n^2) by using the two-pointer technique to find the first occurrence of each rotation.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves two main steps: sorting the input array and then using the two-pointer technique to find the first occurrence of each rotation. We can achieve this in O(n^2) time complexity and O(1) space complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove the correctness of the solution by showing that it satisfies the following properties: 1) It correctly identifies all duplicate rotations in the input array, and 2) It has a time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.dryRun: Let's consider the following input array: [1, 3, 2, 4, 5, 1]. The solution will first sort the array to get [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Then, it will use the two-pointer technique to find the first occurrence of each rotation.
